description: Microbiome signatures and associations in 8 specific disease conditions

findings.description: Increased Escherichia-Shigella, Parasutterella, Blautia, and Romboutsia; decreased Faecalibacterium, Bacteroides, Prevotella, and Bifidobacterium
findings.mechanism: Increased intestinal permeability (elevated serum zonulin); reduced hydrogen sulfide production via attenuated dissimilatory sulfate reduction pathway
findings.evidence_strength: High - multiple cross-sectional studies (Ishaq 2017, Zhao 2018, Liu 2022) plus causal FMT evidence in mice (Zhang 2023)

findings.description: FMT from HT patients worsens autoimmune thyroiditis in mice through reduced hydrogen sulfide production
findings.mechanism: Altered DSR pathway reduces H2S production; H2S deficiency promotes Th1 and Th17 cell differentiation and increases thyroiditis score
findings.evidence_strength: Very High - causal evidence from FMT mouse model with mechanistic validation
findings.cautions: Mouse model of experimentally-induced autoimmune thyroiditis (EAT), not spontaneous HT; may not fully recapitulate human disease

findings.description: Significantly depleted microbial richness and butyrate-producing bacteria; reduced diversity despite normal E. coli gut abundance
findings.mechanism: Broader dysbiosis affecting systemic immunity rather than simple uropathogen overgrowth; impaired short-chain fatty acid production compromises intestinal barrier and regulatory T cell development
findings.evidence_strength: High - year-long longitudinal multi-omics study (Worby 2022, n=15 rUTI, n=16 controls)

findings.description: Even 1% relative gut abundance of Escherichia is an independent risk factor for bacteriuria and UTI; strain-level continuity between gut and urinary tract

microbial_signature.enriched_features: Antimicrobial resistance genes (ARG) enriched in UTI microbiomes

findings.mechanism: Uropathogenic E. coli (UPEC) strains can asymptomatically colonize both gut and urinary tract; post-antibiotic blooms facilitate UPEC expansion and recurrence
findings.evidence_strength: High - multi-center longitudinal study (Choi 2024, n=125) confirms rUTI microbiome enrichment for ARG; kidney transplant cohort (Magruder 2019, n=168) demonstrates prospective risk

findings.description: Bidirectional relationship: iron supplementation alters colonic microflora (suppresses Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium, promotes pathogens); dysbiosis may impair iron absorption
findings.mechanism: Iron modifies colonic pH and redox potential, selecting for pathogenic taxa; dysbiotic microbiota impairs iron absorption via reduced beneficial bacteria
findings.evidence_strength: Moderate - limited direct human evidence linking dysbiotic pattern to IDA as primary cause; evidence primarily from iron supplementation effects
findings.cautions: Direct human evidence linking specific dysbiosis patterns to IDA etiology remains thin; most data describes secondary effects of iron treatment on microbiota

findings.description: Increased gram-negative bacteria (Bacteroides, E. coli, Staphylococcus) and decreased Bifidobacterium, Lactobacillus, and Faecalibacterium prausnitzii
findings.mechanism: Gram-negative bacteria produce or metabolize gluten peptides to immunogenic forms; dysbiosis persists even on gluten-free diet (GFD), suggesting non-dietary component
findings.evidence_strength: High - consistent across multiple reviews and cross-sectional studies (Girbovan 2017, Marasco 2016, Cénit 2015, Rossi 2023)

findings.description: Pseudomonas aeruginosa synergistically escalates gluten-induced intestinal inflammation; Proteobacteria expansion enhances gluten immunopathology in mice
findings.mechanism: PA enzymes may generate immunogenic gluten peptides; Proteobacteria-enriched microbiota (induced by antibiotics) amplifies intestinal inflammation and intraepithelial lymphocyte responses to gluten
findings.evidence_strength: High - mouse model demonstrating causal role of microbiota composition (Galipeau 2015); germ-free and SPF mouse comparison shows clean microbiota attenuates gluten pathology
findings.cautions: Mouse model data; PA effect described mechanistically but tissue-specific localization not confirmed in human studies

findings.description: Reduced Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus; increased E. coli, Clostridium perfringens, and elevated Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes ratio independently associated with 3-fold higher risk of postoperative diarrhea
findings.mechanism: Cholecystectomy disrupts bile acid enterohepatic circulation, which selectively shapes microbial composition; dysbiosis-associated reduced barrier function and increased intestinal inflammation
findings.evidence_strength: Moderate-High - retrospective case-control (Li 2026, n=75) with microbiota profiling; systematic review (Kurbonnazarova 2024) across 13 studies confirms dysbiosis signature

findings.description: Cholecystectomy decreases Bifidobacterium breve and increases Ruminococcus gnavus with altered bile acid metabolism (elevated GUDCA/TUDCA), disrupting FXR signaling and promoting colorectal tumorigenesis
findings.mechanism: Altered bile acid metabolism impairs farnesoid X receptor (FXR) signaling, disrupts FXR/β-catenin interaction, exacerbates intestinal inflammation and neoplastic progression
findings.evidence_strength: Very High - mechanistic proof via FXR agonist (obeticholic acid) prevention of cholecystectomy-related tumorigenesis; metagenomic + metabolomic + functional validation
findings.cautions: Mouse tumor models (AOM/DSS and APCmin/+); human epidemiological association with CRC exists but causality confirmed only in preclinical models

findings.description: Reduced microbial diversity, lower Bifidobacteria, Faecalibacteria, and Clostridia, with higher Escherichia and Enterococcaceae; dysbiosis likely precedes allergy development
findings.mechanism: Early-life dysbiosis impairs regulatory T cell development via reduced short-chain fatty acid production; compromised intestinal barrier function and altered oral tolerance
findings.evidence_strength: High - prospective cohort data suggesting dysbiosis precedes allergic sensitization; consistent across multiple studies (Mares 2025 review of 31 studies)

findings.description: 1.5-year-old food-allergic children show increased Escherichia and decreased Bacteroides, Oscillibacter, and Ruminococcus with predicted increases in intestinal pathogenicity gene expression
findings.mechanism: Pathogenic taxa produce virulence factors that compromise intestinal epithelial integrity; loss of commensal taxa impairs immune tolerance and SCFA production
findings.evidence_strength: High - recent cohort study (Hara 2024, n=29 food-allergic, n=19 healthy controls, age 1.5 years) with functional prediction analysis

findings.description: Reduced Prevotellaceae, Ruminococcus, Faecalibacterium, and F. prausnitzii (health-associated taxa); significantly increased histamine-secreting bacteria including Staphylococcus, Proteus, Enterobacteriaceae, C. perfringens, and E. faecalis
findings.mechanism: Greater abundance of histaminogenic bacteria leads to higher gut histamine production, exceeding the degradation capacity of diamine oxidase (DAO), resulting in systemic histamine accumulation
findings.evidence_strength: Moderate - one of few direct sequencing studies in histamine intolerance (Sánchez-Pérez 2022, n=25 histamine intolerance, n=26 healthy controls); mechanism plausible but human causality not yet proven
findings.cautions: DAO deficiency can be genetic, pharmacological, or pathological; dysbiosis may be cause or consequence; animal studies supporting histaminogenic potential exist but human intervention trials lacking

nu14091774.authors: Sònia Sánchez-Pérez, Oriol Comas-Basté, Adriana Duelo, M. T. Veciana-Nogués, M. Berlanga, M. Latorre-Moratalla, M. Vidal-Carou

notes: All 8 conditions share overlapping dysbiotic features: loss of diversity, depletion of SCFA-producers (Faecalibacterium, Bifidobacterium), and enrichment of Proteobacteria
HT and cholecystectomy have strongest causal evidence (FMT and mechanistic studies)
Food allergy and UTI have prospective/longitudinal data suggesting dysbiosis may precede/predict disease
Celiac and post-bariatric remain primarily cross-sectional; bariatric literature underrepresented in current search
IDA has weakest direct microbiome evidence; most data describes secondary effects of iron supplementation
Histamine intolerance represents emerging area with limited human studies
